title: A 'Living Fossil' With a Twisted Jaw: This 275-Million-Year-Old Tetrapod Defies Explanation
summary: Deep in a Brazilian forest, in a dry riverbed, paleontologists made a discovery that left them scratching their heads for years. They found a six-inch fossil jawbone—and then eight more just like it—from an animal that lived 275 million years ago. But these weren't ordinary jaws. They were twisted, with teeth pointing outward and sideways instead of up. "The jaw has this weird twist that drove us crazy trying to figure it out," says Jason Pardo, lead author of a new study in Proceedings of the Royal Society B. "We were wondering if it was some kind of deformation." But with nine specimens, all showing the same twist, the team concluded it was a real anatomical feature. The animal, named Tanyka amnicola (from the Guaraní word for "jaw" and Latin for "living by the river"), belonged to an ancient lineage of tetrapods—four-limbed vertebrates. Even in its own time, it was a holdover, a "living fossil" akin to today's platypus, which retains egg-laying while most mammals evolved live birth. "Tanyka is a little like a platypus," Pardo explains. "It was a remaining member of the stem tetrapod lineage, even after newer, more modern tetrapods evolved." The jaw's inner surface, covered in small teeth called denticles, faced upward, forming a rough grinding pad. Scientists suspect the upper jaw had a matching surface, allowing the teeth to rasp against each other like a cheese grater. This suggests Tanyka was an herbivore, a surprising twist since most stem tetrapods were carnivorous. "Based on its teeth, we think that Tanyka ate plants at least some of the time," says co-author Juan Carlos Cisneros. The rest of Tanyka's body remains a mystery. Without a skull or skeleton attached to the jaws, researchers can only guess it resembled a salamander with a longer snout, possibly reaching three feet in length. The surrounding rock indicates it lived in freshwater lakes. The discovery sheds light on the early Permian Period in Gondwana, the southern supercontinent. "The Pedra de Fogo Formation in Brazil is one of the only windows we have into Gondwana's animals during this time," says co-author Ken Angielczyk. "Tanyka is telling us about how this community actually worked, who was eating what." As scientists continue to hunt for more complete fossils, Tanyka stands as a bizarre reminder that evolution often experiments with strange designs—some of which, like a sideways-toothed jaw, can stump even the experts.
why_it_matters: This twisted jaw reminds us that evolution often experiments with bizarre body plans; solving such puzzles helps scientists reconstruct Earth's deep past and understand the origins of today's animal groups.

keywords.explanation: Scientists who study fossils to learn about ancient life.
A vertebrate animal with four limbs, or descended from four-limbed ancestors.
An early branch of the tetrapod family that existed before modern groups like reptiles and mammals appeared.
Small, tooth-like bumps that can form a rough surface, like sandpaper.
An animal that eats only plants.
An ancient supercontinent that later broke into South America, Africa, Australia, and Antarctica.

questions.question: What discovery puzzled paleontologists in Brazil?
options: A fossil jawbone with twisted teeth
questions.correct_answer: A fossil jawbone with twisted teeth

questions.question: How many identical twisted jaw fossils did the team find?
What did the denticles on Tanyka's jaw likely form?
What surprising diet does Tanyka's jaw suggest?
Which ancient landmass did Tanyka live on?
Why did scientists initially think the twisted jaw might be a deformation?
options: It was too small to be real

background_read: The fossil came from the Pedra de Fogo Formation in Brazil, a geological layer famous for preserving animals from the Permian Period (299–252 million years ago, before dinosaurs).
During that time, most land on Earth was combined into the southern supercontinent Gondwana, which later broke apart into South America, Africa, Australia, and Antarctica.
The study was published in Proceedings of the Royal Society B, a scientific journal that has been sharing major biology discoveries since the 1800s.
'Stem tetrapods' are early four-limbed animals that branched off before the common ancestor of modern reptiles, birds, and mammals; they help scientists understand how vertebrates moved onto land.
Tanyka is called a 'living fossil' because its lineage held onto ancient traits even after newer tetrapod groups emerged, much like today's platypus still lays eggs.
Article_Structure: LEAD: Discovery of nine twisted jawbones leaves paleontologists baffled
└─ QUOTE: 'The jaw has this weird twist that drove us crazy'
KEY FIND: New species Tanyka amnicola, a 275-million-year-old stem tetrapod
├─ NAME MEANING: 'Tanyka' (jaw in Guaraní) + 'living by river' in Latin
├─ ANATOMY: Teeth point outward, inner denticles form grinding pad
└─ DIET: Herbivore—surprising, as most stem tetrapods were carnivorous
TENSION: Initial suspicion of deformation resolved by nine identical specimens
CONTEXT: Early Permian Period in Gondwana, Pedra de Fogo Formation, Brazil
└─ IMPORTANCE: Rare window into Gondwanan ecosystem and food webs
OPEN QUESTION: The rest of the body is unknown—only jaws found

perspectives.description: Paleontologists gain a rare glimpse into the animals of Gondwana, filling major gaps in the fossil record. The twisted jaw reveals that early herbivory evolved in unexpected ways, enriching our understanding of ancient ecosystems.

The fossil is incomplete, leaving huge gaps about Tanyka's whole body and lifestyle. Until more complete skeletons are found, the reconstruction remains speculative, and its exact place in the evolutionary tree is uncertain.

Tanyka's 'living fossil' status highlights how evolution can preserve ancient body plans even as newer groups arise. This pattern, like the platypus retaining reptilian egg-laying, shows that survival doesn't always require the latest innovations—niches can be filled by odd holdovers.

Future expeditions in the Pedra de Fogo Formation may uncover skulls or limbs of Tanyka, providing clues about how stem tetrapods adapted to plant eating. Such finds could reshape theories about the transition from aquatic to fully terrestrial lifestyles in the Permian.
